---
title: "DESeq vs DEXSeq"
author: "Haeun Lee"
date: "2026-02-11"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```


DESeq2 and DEXSeq are closely related (DEXSeq actually builds on DESeq2 machinery), but they answer different biological questions and fit different models.

⸻

# What DESeq2 Does

Biological question

Is the overall gene expression level different between conditions?

It tests:

Gene A (total counts across all exons)
Condition 1 vs Condition 2

Unit of analysis:

🧬 Gene

Data structure:

genes × samples

Model (simplified):

\log(\mu_{ij}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot condition_j

Where:
	•	i = gene
	•	j = sample

Output:
	•	log2 fold change per gene
	•	Wald test p-values
	•	adjusted p-values

⸻

# What DEXSeq Does

Biological question

Is the relative exon usage within a gene different between conditions?

It tests:

Within Gene A:
Is exon 2 used differently between conditions?

This detects:
	•	alternative splicing
	•	exon skipping
	•	differential transcript structure

⸻

Unit of analysis:

🧬 Exon (within gene)

Data structure:

exons × samples

BUT crucially:
Exons are modeled relative to their gene.

⸻

# The Core Statistical Difference

This is the key distinction.

⸻

DESeq2 model (gene-level)

For each gene:

count_{gene} \sim condition

Simple.

⸻

DEXSeq model (exon-level interaction model)

For each exon:

count_{exon} \sim sample + exon + condition:exon

The important term is:

condition:exon

That interaction tests:

Does this exon behave differently across conditions relative to other exons in the same gene?

This is not testing total expression.
It tests proportion usage changes.

⸻

# What DEXSeq is Really Testing

DEXSeq controls for overall gene expression automatically.

Even if:
	•	the whole gene doubles in expression

DEXSeq will NOT call it significant
unless:
	•	exon usage proportions change.

⸻

# Pipeline Differences

DESeq2 pipeline:

dds <- DESeq(dds)
res <- results(dds)

Internally:
	•	estimate size factors
	•	estimate dispersions
	•	fit NB GLM
	•	Wald test

⸻

DEXSeq pipeline:

dxd <- estimateSizeFactors(dxd)
dxd <- estimateDispersions(dxd)
dxd <- testForDEU(dxd)
dxd <- estimateExonFoldChanges(dxd)

Internally:
	•	same NB framework
	•	but larger design matrix
	•	exon-level interaction model
	•	likelihood ratio test (LRT), not Wald test

⸻

# Statistical Test Type

Method	Default Test
DESeq2	Wald test
DEXSeq	Likelihood ratio test

DEXSeq compares:

full model (with exon:condition interaction)
vs
reduced model (without interaction)


⸻

# Interpretation Difference

DESeq2 significant gene:

“Gene is upregulated”

DEXSeq significant exon:

“Exon usage changed”
→ alternative splicing event

The gene might not be DE at all.

⸻

# Data Scale Difference

DESeq2:
	•	~20,000 genes

DEXSeq:
	•	~200,000+ exons
	•	much heavier computation

⸻

# When to Use Which

Question	Tool
Is gene expression different?	DESeq2
Is alternative splicing different?	DEXSeq
Both?	Run both separately


⸻

# Conceptual Summary

DESeq2:

Tests vertical change (expression amount)

DEXSeq:

Tests horizontal change (internal exon structure)

⸻

DEXSeq uses DESeq2’s:
	•	size factor estimation
	•	dispersion estimation
	•	NB model framework

But wraps them in a different design and hypothesis test.

⸻
